does: This paper distinguishes two separate dimensions of the AI alignment problem: direct alignment, which ensures an AI system accomplishes the goals of its operator, and social alignment, which addresses effects on broader groups and society, including externalities imposed on others. The authors argue these problems require fundamentally different solution approaches. Direct alignment focuses on robust implementation mechanisms to ensure operators achieve their intended objectives through the system. Social alignment, by contrast, stems primarily from conflicts between individual operator goals and collective group-level welfare, requiring governance interventions to mediate these tensions. The paper thus reframes alignment not as a single unified problem but as a two-axis challenge with distinct technical and institutional dimensions.
connects: This work builds on foundational AI alignment literature that has primarily focused on value specification and goal representation, extending those frameworks to explicitly address distributional and societal concerns often implicit in earlier work. It connects to research on AI governance and policy that recognizes alignment involves more than technical robustness, drawing on insights from institutional economics and collective action problems. The paper also engages with growing literature on AI externalities and dual-use concerns, particularly work examining how AI systems affect non-users and unintended stakeholders. It situates itself within broader alignment scholarship that acknowledges alignment depends on normative choices about whose values matter and under what decision-making structures.
enables: This framework enables future research to tackle the alignment problem with appropriate tools matched to each dimension. For direct alignment, it points toward improved technical work on interpretability, robustness, and adversarial testing specific to operator-system goal congruence. For social alignment, it opens research agendas in AI governance design, including mechanism design for multi-stakeholder AI deployment, regulatory frameworks for managing externalities, and norm-setting processes for AI developers and operators. The distinction also enables more productive conversations about alignment-policy interactions, identifying where technical solutions suffice versus where governance and norm-building become essential. This could facilitate better allocation of research effort and clearer institutional design for responsible AI development.
